title: AI executives push back on the sell-off: demand is 'almost unlimited,' with energy the real constraint
subtitle: Pat Gelsinger, Rebellions' Sungyun Park, and Lumentum's leadership told CNBC that neither the Meta Compute announcement nor the neocloud rout reflects the real order book, which they say is booked out several years.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: In interviews published by CNBC on July 12, several AI infrastructure executives pushed back on the interpretation that recent chip-stock volatility signals softening AI demand. Pat Gelsinger, the former Intel CEO now a general partner at Playground Global, said demand is 'almost unlimited' and that energy availability — not silicon supply, not capital, not customer intent — is 'the only real limiter.' Sungyun Park, CEO of Korean AI-chip startup Rebellions, said 'AI infrastructure momentum [is] still huge' and rejected the framing that hyperscalers are visibly overinvesting. Lumentum, which supplies photonics and optical products used in data-center connectivity, said its products are sold out for the next five years and that it is racing to add capacity.

The commentary is a direct response to two weeks of turbulence triggered by Meta's disclosure that it will resell excess AI computing capacity through a new Meta Compute business. That announcement sent CoreWeave down 11–14% in a single session, Nebius down 17%, IREN down about 6%, and dragged Asian memory names Samsung and SK Hynix down between 9% and 15% at the peak — market moves that suggested investors were treating 'compute is perpetually scarce' as a broken assumption. Elon Musk's xAI has also rented out excess capacity this year, reinforcing the perception that even the most aggressive scaler is finding room in its own build-out.

The two views are less contradictory than they look. If demand truly is booked years out — Lumentum's five-year backlog is the concrete data point — then Meta and xAI reselling short-term slack does not change the long-term shortage; it just smooths a lumpy delivery schedule. But if the marginal AI workload is more elastic than executives think, the price of GPU-hours could fall meaningfully once large sellers arrive, and the neocloud pure-plays whose entire pitch is 'faster than AWS' lose their pricing premium first. The next quarterly earnings from CoreWeave and Nebius, and any commentary from Meta about actual Meta Compute pricing, will settle which story the numbers are telling.

Takeaway for learners: 'unlimited demand' and 'oversupply' can both be true depending on the time horizon. A useful habit when reading these stories is to ask which time frame the person is describing — a two-year contract, a spot-market price, a five-year backlog — and whether the market is reacting to the same one. Most 'AI bubble' arguments and most 'AI shortage' arguments talk past each other because they are measuring different windows.
